---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.size70[**Today's Agenda**]

.size50[
1. Four Principles of Data Analysis

2. Report on your early findings

3. Audit the data
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Review assignment submissions

2. Publish next discussion

3. ADD spreadsheet snapshot of chosen data to slides



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle, size35

### Report 1: Analyzing our Outcome Variable(s)

1. .textblue[**What is it and why is this project important?**]

2. .textblue[**How confident should we be in the methodology?**]

3. What do the observations currently show us?

4. How are these measures changing across time?

5. Overall, how confident can we be when using this data to explore the world?

???

Our work this week continues to focus on these two elements of the report.
- With a little bit of data analysis mixed-in.

Today's work is the logical extension of our codebook analyses.
- How does the actual data look?

Be sure to TAKE NOTES as we work and save them!
- You should ABSOLUTELY be writing this report as we go!

### Make sense?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[**For Today**]

.size45[
1. Wheelan chapter 1 "What's the point?"

2. **Submit to Canvas before class**: 

Start exploring the dataset in Excel and find us something interesting/puzzling/surprising or simply something you learned
]

???

Today we finally get to dig into the data and get our hands dirty!

<br>

### In broad strokes, how was your first dive into our data using Excel?
#### - Not yet about what you found but HOW you found the tool and the unguided exploration?

<br>

Now, before we discuss what you found I want to set you up with four important lessons to keep in mind when working with data.

- Commit these to memory!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**Four Key Principles of Data Analysis**]

.size45[
1. Data analysis is .textred[**not**] a linear process

2. Always .textblue[**tidy**] your data before exploring it

3. Variable .textblue[**type**] determines the .textblue[**tool**]

4. .textblue[**Validity**] and .textblue[**reliability**] come first!
]

???

Think of these like guiding rules to get us off on the right foot.

- Let's step through each one.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**1. Data analysis is .textred[not] a linear process**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_2-Analysis_Process.png")
```

???

The first principle of data analysis is to recognize that it is NOT a linear process.
- The day-to-day work of a data scientist almost never looks like a straightforward path through a puzzle.


Hadley Wickham, a big name in R programming, proposes this as one way to think about being a data scientist.
- Each step represents a series of tasks you will need to do in order to answer questions using data.

<br>

Important sub-ideas here:

1. Finding, cleaning and organizing data happens before anything you might think of as "doing statistics."
    - I spend the bulk of my time as a quants researcher in these stages.

2. Developing your understanding is a process of playing with data, **NOT knowing the right thing to do from the get-go.**
    - Your job is to learn about the world and that comes from exploration.
    
    - Don't freeze-up or get discouraged when looking at new data
    
    - Just start doing stuff with it and see what happens! THEN reflect on what you made and go from there.
    
<br>

### Make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**2. .textblue[Tidy] your data before exploring it**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_2-tidy.png")
```

???

The second principle of data analysis is that you MUST tidy your data BEFORE you can do anything useful with it.

- Essentially, all stats packages and even Excel assume your data is tidy before the built-in tools can be used.

<br>

This means:

1. Columns are variables
    - e.g. separate concepts to be measured
    
2. Rows as observations
    - Each row refers to one observations
    - e.g. person, group, state, etc.

3. Each cell as a separate data point.

<br>

This is our goal. Take messy data and get it into this format.

### Everybody have this written down?

- **SLIDE**: Let's look at some examples.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle, center

## Freedom House: "Freedom in the World"

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-FH_Untidy1.png")
```

???

Freedom House publishes data on "freedom" around the world.

- The status column includes the frequently referenced three level cataegorical variable: Free, Partly Free and Not Free

- This is how they distribute the data for the current or recent years.

<br>

### How does this do on the three rules of tidy data?

1. (Columns are NOT yet variables: 2 levels of header)

2. (Rows ARE observations)
    - Observations here are state-year so each row needs to be one state at one year

3. (Each cell is a separate data point.)

<br>

**SLIDE**: This is a SUPER easy clean-up job.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle, center

### Freedom House: "Freedom in the World"

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_2-tidy_fh1.png")
```

.center[.size55[&#x2193;]]

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_2-tidy_fh2.png")
```

???

### Make sense?

1. Each column is now a separate variable

2. Each row is a separate observation

3. Each cell is a distinct data point




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle, center

## Freedom House: "Freedom in the World"

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-FH_Untidy.png")
```

???

Now, if what you want is historical Freedom House data you get this.

- All of the information is useful but this is definitely not tidy.

<br>

### How does this do on the three rules of tidy data?

1. (Columns are NOT variables)
2. (Rows are MULTIPLE observations
    - Observations here are state-year so each row needs to be one state at one year
3. (Each cell is a data point)
    - Ignoring the messy three column header it gets close on this one

<br>

### What would we need to do to fix it?
### - What should/could a tidy result look like?
- *ON BOARD*
    - Country, Region, C/T, Edition, Year, Status, ...

- (**SLIDE**: Let's fix it!)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle, center


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-FH_Untidy_Small.png")
```

.center[.size55[&#x2193;]]

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '78%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-FH_Tidy2.png")
```

???

### Make sense?

1. (Columns are variables)

2. (Rows are country-year observations

3. (Each cell is a data point)

<br>

### Questions on what tidy data looks like?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**2. .textblue[Tidy] your data before exploring it**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-GII_Untidy.png")
```

???

One of your jobs for Friday will be to take our current dataset and tidy it!

### Questions on that task?

<br>

*optional: Let the class brainstorm how to do this*
1. ALWAYS keep your working copy AND the original data!
    - This way you keep your record of "science" clean and repeatable.
2. Collapse all column headers so that each column is one variable
3. Remove empty rows, e.g. "VERY HIGH HUMAN DEVELOPMENT", because they aren't observations
4. Remove notes from the bottom of the sheet
5. Remove aggregated data, e.g. world and region averages



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**3. Variable .textblue[type] determines the .textblue[tool]**]

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_2-levels_measurement.png")
```

???

The third principle of data analysis is that variable type determines the tool to use on it.

- All "data" can be classified by its type.

- The most important distinction is this middle row.
    - Is the variable measured using numbers or words?

- Each type can then be broken down further, but the big key is numbers vs words.

### Looking at our data for today, which variables are numbers and which are words?

- Be careful! Very often numbers represent categories of words!
    - This is why you have to review the codebook.

<br>

**SLIDE**: The handy thing is that once you can identify variables by type, you know exactly what kinds of analyses to perform!





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_2-chart_types.jpg')
background-size: 80%
background-class: center

???

Don't let this picture overwhelm you!
- I don't think the four words here in the middle are the clearest way to do this.

The logic here is what matters, not these specific recommendations.
- The lesson here is that tool selection depends on variable type.

<br>

Example: Are you trying to visualize the distribution of a numeric variable? 
- Make a histogram!

Example: Are trying to visualize a relationship between two numeric variables?
- Make a scatterplot!

<br>

A big part of your job early this semester will be organizing your notes around each type.
- e.g. "When I have a continuous variable I can..."

Once you learn to think this way, data analysis starts to look like cooking with recipes.
- You learn to describe what you want to make and our texts and notes will tell you how to do it!





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**4. Evaluate Validity & Reliability**]

<br>

```{r, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_2-valid_and_reliable.png")
```

???

The fourth principle of data analysis is that you must begin every analysis by thinking critically about the quality of your data in terms of validity and reliability.

- It's so important we spent the first week of our class talking about measurement and uncertainty! 

- AND we used this kind of figure to evaluate the codebook too!

<br>

Validity: "Answers correspond to what they are intended to measure"
- Ruler includes actual inches on it that correspond to accepted standards.

Reliability: "Providing consistent measures in comparable situations"
- Instructions clear enough so each person uses the ruler in the same way.

<br>

This is absolutely the MOST important piece of analyzing data and it simply isn't done enough in stats type classes.

- We get so excited about methods and fancy pictures we forget that our knowledge is constrained by the data we are using.

Remember, all data includes uncertainty.

- If the data project does a bad job or is not transparent about how it handles all of this, then I don't care what they think about the development of some complex societal dynamic.

### Questions on this?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size50[**Four Key Principles of Data Analysis**]

.size45[
1. Data analysis is .textred[**not**] a linear process

2. Always .textblue[**tidy**] your data before exploring it

3. Variable .textblue[**type**] determines the .textblue[**tool**]

4. .textblue[**Validity**] and .textblue[**reliability**] come first!
]

???

### Any questions on the four principles underpinning our work as data scientists?

<br>

Let's start our data work today by using some simple Excel tools to examine the data.

- I think there's a lot of value in getting a sense for the shape of the data by looking at it in a spreadsheet before starting the more serious work.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-excel_dangerous.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Important word of caution!

- Excel is a VERY dangerous tool because it wants to help you do "analysis"

- That's very kind of it to offer, but 

- Unfortunately, it is VERY, VERY stupid.

<br>

**SLIDE**: For example...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-excel_danger2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Beware Excel's auto-formatting features!

- As I said, like a drunk puppy Excel is desperate to help but has no idea what you're trying to do or how to actually help.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/03_2-Excel_Finger_Slip.png')
background-size: 95%
background-position: center
class: slideblue

???

There are so many examples

1. Each cell of the data can be edited (intentionally or by accident) and those changes don't get recorded or flagged anywhere.
    - A finger slip can completely transform your records and you would never know it!
    
2. The sort features are confusing and sometimes lead to sorting only one column rather than the whole sheet which breaks the data at the observation level.

3. The autofilter is handy for extracting subsets of the data but it's very easy to forget it is "on" and suddenly your analyses exhibit serious selection bias.

<br>

Bottom line, doing "science" requires being transparent in your choices and creating a record of them for others to review.

- Excel is a total failure at this and that is why it is completely inappropriate for doing science.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/03_2-GII_Untidy.png")
```

???

Let's explore the data (CAREFULLY) in Excel.

### 1. How are the observations organized?

<br>

### 2. Do we understand all of the measures here and can we connect them back to the codebook?

<br>

### 3. What did you find from your pre-class explorations?
- *Share interesting/puzzling/surprising findings*
- *ON BOARD*

<br>

*Use these observations as a direct segue into the audit!*

Let's now audit these surprising findings!

- How do these actual observations help us think critically about the codebook?

- Are the measures all plausible?

- Are there serious missing data issues?










---

background-image: url('libs/Images/03_2-Audit2.png')
background-size: 50%
background-position: right
class: middle, slideblue

.pull-left[
.center[
.size45[**Audit the Data**]

.size40[
Can you find source(s) that confirm this observation?
]]]

???

Can you find some other source that allows us to confirm or question the observation you selected?

- An external dataset also aiming to measure the same thing?

- A news story describing the observation in line with this measurement?

### Does that make sense?

- This might not work but I'd like us to try it!

<br>

*Divy out the observations*

So, everybody pick one and spend the next X minutes trying to audit the observation.

<br>

### What did you try and what did you find?

#### - Approaches tried?
#### - Sources found? Independence?

<br>

### Bottom line, how does your audit impact your level of confidence in the validity and reliability of this data set?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_cubes_lighter3.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size55[**For Wednesday**]

.size40[
Installing and configuring R / RStudio

1. Tidy our new dataset

2. Long and Teetor (2019) Sections 1.1 and 1.2

3. Healy (2019) Sections 2.1 - 2.4

4. Bring your computer (optional)
]

???

If you would like to be able to work on your personal computer this semester, bring it to class on Wednesday and we'll spend some time getting everybody set-up!

<br>

Even if you'll be working on the lab computers we will spend class time Wednesday configuring RStudio and getting comfortable using it.


### Questions on the assignment?
